content.Text: [Wed 2026-04-22 22:21 PDT] Create an Interactive Steiner Tree Finder tool at `docs/steiner-tree.html`. This is a self-contained HTML page for the GraphVisual project. ## What is a Steiner Tree? Given a weighted graph and a set of "terminal" nodes, the Steiner tree is the minimum-weight tree that connects all terminals. It may include additional "Steiner" nodes. This is NP-hard, so use heuristic approaches. ## Requirements ### Architecture - Single self-contained HTML file at `docs/steiner-tree.html` - Uses `` for base theming (dark theme with CSS variables like --bg, --surface, --border, --accent, --text, --text-muted, --surface-hover, --accent-hover) - All JS inline in a `
I’m inspecting the existing `docs` tools to match the sidebar and page structure, then I’ll build `docs/steiner-tree.html` as a single self-contained page without touching other files.
I found the docs set. `rg` is not installed here, so I’m reading a few existing tool pages directly to copy the sidebar and layout conventions before I write the new page.

I have enough structure to mirror the layout. I’m checking one of the newer docs pages for the fuller sidebar list so the new Tools entry lands in the same navigation pattern rather than the older shorter variants.

ℹ️ About

A Minimum Spanning Tree connects all nodes with the minimum total edge weight and no cycles.

  • Prim's — grows tree from a start node, always adding the cheapest crossing edge.
  • Kruskal's — sorts all edges by weight, adds them if they don't form a cycle (Union-Find).

🌲 Treewidth Analyzer

The TreewidthAnalyzer computes upper and lower bounds on the treewidth of a graph and produces a tree decomposition. Treewidth is a fundamental graph parameter that measures how "tree-like" a graph is.

Why Treewidth Matters

  • Algorithm design: Many NP-hard problems (vertex cover, independent set, graph coloring, Hamiltonian path) can be solved in linear time on graphs of bounded treewidth via dynamic programming on tree decompositions.
  • Structure insight: Low treewidth reveals that a graph has a recursive, tree-like structure that can be exploited.
  • Parameterized complexity: Treewidth is the most studied structural parameter in parameterized algorithms.

Computed Metrics

MetricDescription
Upper BoundWidth of the best tree decomposition found via greedy elimination (min-degree and min-fill heuristics).
Lower BoundMaximum of degeneracy (k-core number) and minor-min-width heuristic.
Tree DecompositionSet of bags (vertex subsets) connected by tree edges, satisfying the tree decomposition properties.
Classificationtw=0 → independent set, tw=1 → forest, tw=2 → series-parallel, tw=3 → many problems linear-time.

Usage

// Full analysis with bounds
TreewidthAnalyzer.TreewidthResult result = TreewidthAnalyzer.analyze(graph);
System.out.printf("Treewidth bounds: [%d, %d]%n",
    result.getLowerBound(), result.getUpperBound());
System.out.printf("Exact? %s%n", result.isExact());

// Access the tree decomposition
TreewidthAnalyzer.TreeDecomposition decomp = result.getDecomposition();
for (TreewidthAnalyzer.Bag bag : decomp.getBags()) {
    System.out.println(bag);
}

// Or get a formatted report
System.out.println(TreewidthAnalyzer.report(graph));

Algorithms

🌲 Treewidth Analyzer

The TreewidthAnalyzer computes upper and lower bounds on the treewidth of a graph and produces a tree decomposition. Treewidth is a fundamental graph parameter that measures how "tree-like" a graph is.

Why Treewidth Matters

  • Algorithm design: Many NP-hard problems (vertex cover, independent set, graph coloring, Hamiltonian path) can be solved in linear time on graphs of bounded treewidth via dynamic programming on tree decompositions.
  • Structure insight: Low treewidth reveals that a graph has a recursive, tree-like structure that can be exploited.
  • Parameterized complexity: Treewidth is the most studied structural parameter in parameterized algorithms.

Computed Metrics

MetricDescription
Upper BoundWidth of the best tree decomposition found via greedy elimination (min-degree and min-fill heuristics).
Lower BoundMaximum of degeneracy (k-core number) and minor-min-width heuristic.
Tree DecompositionSet of bags (vertex subsets) connected by tree edges, satisfying the tree decomposition properties.
Classificationtw=0 → independent set, tw=1 → forest, tw=2 → series-parallel, tw=3 → many problems linear-time.

Usage

// Full analysis with bounds
TreewidthAnalyzer.TreewidthResult result = TreewidthAnalyzer.analyze(graph);
System.out.printf("Treewidth bounds: [%d, %d]%n",
    result.getLowerBound(), result.getUpperBound());
System.out.printf("Exact? %s%n", result.isExact());

// Access the tree decomposition
TreewidthAnalyzer.TreeDecomposition decomp = result.getDecomposition();
for (TreewidthAnalyzer.Bag bag : decomp.getBags()) {
    System.out.println(bag);
}

// Or get a formatted report
System.out.println(TreewidthAnalyzer.report(graph));

Algorithms

Upper Bounds (Greedy Elimination)

Two heuristics are used, and the best result is kept:

  • Min-degree: Always eliminates the vertex with the fewest remaining neighbors. Fast and often effective.
  • Min-fill: Eliminates the vertex whose elimination adds the fewest new edges. Usually produces tighter bounds but is slower.

Each elimination ordering produces a valid tree decomposition. The width is the size of the largest bag minus one.

Lower Bounds

  • Degeneracy: The maximum k-core number. Always ≤ treewidth.
  • Minor-min-width: Iteratively contracts edges of minimum-degree vertices, tracking the maximum minimum degree seen. Often tighter than degeneracy alone.

Known Treewidths

Graph FamilyTreewidth
Trees, forests1
Series-parallel graphs≤ 2
Outerplanar graphs≤ 2
Halin graphs3
Planar graphsO(√n)
Complete graph Knn − 1
Grid graph √n × √n√n

Output Example

=== Treewidth Analysis Report ===

Vertices: 8    Edges: 10

--- Bounds ---
Lower bound: 2 (degeneracy)
Upper bound: 3 (min-fill elimination)

--- Classification ---
Treewidth ≤ 3: many NP-hard problems solvable in linear time.

--- Tree Decomposition (8 bags, 7 tree edges) ---
Bag sizes: min=1, max=4, avg=2.5

First 8 bags:
  Bag 0: {A, B, C}
  Bag 1: {B, C, D}
  Bag 2: {C, D, E, F}
  ...

🛡️ Minimum Vertex Cover Finder Find minimum vertex covers with greedy, exact & König algorithms

A vertex cover of a graph G is a set S of vertices such that every edge has at least one endpoint in S. Finding a minimum vertex cover is NP-hard, but a simple greedy 2-approximation exists. For bipartite graphs, König's theorem gives an exact solution in polynomial time via maximum matching.
